preamble: The change from voiceless dental fricative [θ] to palatal glide [j] would require both a large forward shift in place of articulation (dental → palatal) and a manner change (fricative → approximant). No direct θ→j change is attested as a regular sound change in any documented language family. The well-documented changes from [θ] are: stopping to [t] (th-stopping, as in Caribbean English), fronting to [f] (th-fronting, as in Cockney), and merger with [s] or [h] in various historical shifts. The θ→j direction is not supported by known phonetic pathways and should be treated as hypothetical.

examples.note: Research across English dialects, Celtic languages, Ancient Greek, Caribbean Creoles, and Proto-Semitic found no attestation of a θ→j change as a natural phonological rule. Documented lenitions of θ produce [t], [f], [s], or deletion — not [j]. This transformation should be flagged for removal or replacement with a better-attested near-equivalent.

sources: Wells, J. C. (1982). Accents of English. Cambridge University Press.
Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages. Blackwell.
